title: Kanab/Escalante grazing management : environmental impact statement.

entities.quote: We have reviewed the draft statement and have no comments to offer.
entities.stance_basis: reviewed DEIS but offered no comments

entities.quote: Thank you for considering these comments in preparing the Kanab/Escalante Final Environmental Statement. We hope you will consider them fully along with others that are received and make the necessary changes that will be for the good of all concerned.
entities.stance_basis: requests specific corrections and flexibility provisions as conditions for workable grazing management

entities.quote: It is our opinion that none of the six alternative grazing proposals conform with the multiple use mandate of the Federal Land Policy and Management Act of 1976. Alternative 5, Rangeland Management Recommendation (Preferred Alternative) is a single purpose proposal with only cosmetic differences between it and alternatives 1 (Continuation of Present Management), 4 (Adjustment to Grazing Capacity), and 6 (Livestock Optimization).
entities.stance_basis: none of the six alternatives conform with multiple use mandate; preferred alternative inadequate for wildlife

entities.role: Area Manager, Fish and Wildlife Service, Salt Lake City
entities.quote: It is our opinion that none of the six alternative grazing proposals conform with the multiple use mandate of the Federal Land Policy and Management Act of 1976.
entities.stance_basis: signed letter opposing all six alternatives as non-compliant with multiple use mandate

entities.quote: The whole document is very misleading discriminatory, and biased when judged by lifetime cattlemen like myself. To me it suggests a socialistic form of government when the BLM can come in and take away what has rightfully belonged to the cattlemen since the discovery of this valley.
entities.stance_basis: opposes EIS as misleading, biased, and harmful to cattlemen's livelihoods

entities.quote: Many of us living in these areas feel that it would be criminal for BLM to carry out its proposed grazing cut-backs.
entities.stance_basis: opposes proposed grazing cut-backs as harmful to Kane and Garfield County economies

entities.quote: No single alternative seems to fit the needs of multiple resource enhancement. Alternative 3 gives the needed protection to riparian areas, wildlife and soil resources but may be detrimental to the ranching community... With adequate response to these questions, the combination of alternatives 3, 4 and 5 should be considered as a legitimate alternative.
entities.stance_basis: no single alternative adequate; supports combination of alternatives 3, 4, and 5 with conditions

entities.quote: No single alternative seems to fit the needs of multiple resource enhancement. Alternative 3 gives the needed protection to riparian areas, wildlife and soil resources but may be detrimental to the ranching community.
entities.stance_basis: no single alternative adequate; supports combination of alternatives with conditions including riparian protection

entities.quote: It is our opinion they are only projections and not realistic. They show only a biased phase and for the time and money expended are un-needed expenses.
entities.stance_basis: opposes EIS projections as unrealistic and biased against livestock interests

entities.quote: Our greatest concerns are the projected programs. It is our opinion they are only projections and not realistic. They show only a biased phase and for the time and money expended are un-needed expenses.
entities.stance_basis: opposes EIS projections as unrealistic and biased; concerned about livestock reduction relative to wildlife

entities.role: Cooperative Extension Service, Utah State University / commenter
entities.quote: The analysis carried out in this environmental assessment indicates that excessive dependence is being placed on range survey grazing capacity estimates without adequate consideration of other modifying factors. It is suggested that this trend be altered at the point where decisions are to be made.
entities.stance_basis: supports improved management but conditions acceptance on correcting over-reliance on range survey estimates

entities.entity: Cooperative Extension Service, Utah State University
entities.quote: The analysis carried out in this environmental assessment indicates that excessive dependence is being placed on range survey grazing capacity estimates without adequate consideration of other modifying factors.
entities.stance_basis: supports rangeland management improvement but conditions acceptance on better use of modifying factors beyond survey estimates

entities.quote: THE SEASON NEEDS TO BE FROM JUNE 1 TO OCTOBER 15. PLEASE GIVE FULL CONSIDERATION TO THIS REQUEST.
entities.stance_basis: requests season of use change for Upper North Fork Allotment as condition for workable grazing plan

entities.quote: EPA supports the effort by the BLM to improve rangeland conditions for livestock grazing and other uses.
entities.stance_basis: supports BLM rangeland improvement effort but raises concerns about failure to address saline runoff as a major grazing management objective

entities.quote: Of primary concern to EPA is the failure of your current planning efforts to recognize that reductions in saline runoff should be a major objective of grazing management within the Colorado River basin.
entities.stance_basis: concerned that EIS fails to recognize saline runoff reduction as a major grazing management objective

entities.entity: Department of the Army, Los Angeles District Corps of Engineers
entities.quote: The proposed plan does not conflict with existing or authorized plans of the Corps of Engineers, Los Angeles District. We have no comments on the DEIR.
entities.stance_basis: no conflict with Corps plans; no substantive comments on the DEIS

entities.quote: I do want it reduced as I graye with other Terrettee, I want the range in good condition in my came as of dave to make "my living fell time from limetock. But if the range will carry the allatted catile it would be unwise to keep porting cutting cattle which but the economy our community.
entities.stance_basis: wants range in good condition but opposes further cattle cuts if range can carry allotted cattle; withholds signing new agreement pending promised improvements

entities.role: commenter, businessman and resident of Kane County
entities.quote: In conclusion, the Kanab/Escalante Grazing Management Environmental Impact Statement is an inaccurate, poorly prepared, unfair compilation of paperwork. I recommend that a serious, long look be taken at this document before any decision be made. Of the 6 alternatives suggested in the E.I.S. Statement, I suggest alternative 1.
entities.stance_basis: calls EIS inaccurate and poorly prepared; recommends Alternative 1 and opposes large grazing cuts

entities.entity: Kane County Board of County Commissioners
entities.quote: It is recommended by the Kane County Board of County Commissioners that no cuts should be imposed at all. Rather, the present Allis should be maintained with the understanding that subsequent cuts or additions should be worked out between the individual cattlemen and the Area Manager for the Bureau of Land Management.
entities.stance_basis: recommends no AUM cuts; supports Alternative 1 elements combined with Alternative 6 infrastructure; opposes BLM credibility and VRM classifications

entities.entity: Office of the Governor, State of Utah
entities.quote: Our analysis indicates that there are very few areas in the Kanab-Escalante region where cuts from permitted use are necessary. Historically, ranchers have demonstrated a sense of range capacity and have cut actual use to coincide with changing range conditions.
entities.stance_basis: supports minimal cuts, seasonal flexibility, and range improvements concurrent with any cuts; conditions support on data reliability and rancher consultation

entities.quote: At the same time. I hope BEM will move quickly to provide the range improvements suggested by the statement. Too often, promises of range improvements have not been followed by action.
entities.stance_basis: supports process but conditions credibility on BLM following through on range improvement commitments concurrent with cuts

entities.quote: The State appreciates the magnitude of analyzing livestock grazing on almost three million acres, involving 212 grazing allotments. Generally, the Bureau has done a commendable job in attempting such a large and comprehensive undertaking. However, there are several areas of concern which the following comments address:
entities.stance_basis: generally commends BLM effort but raises multiple concerns about negative framing, survey accuracy, erosion discussion, and economic analysis

entities.quote: The Five County Association of Governments water quality report identifies the Escalante and Kanab drainage system as high priority for control of agricultural nonpoint sources.
entities.stance_basis: identifies Escalante and Kanab drainages as high priority for nonpoint source control; recommends proper grazing use in erosive areas

entities.entity: Kane County and the Escalante Cattlemen's Association
entities.role: rancher associations, participants in budget review
entities.quote: the memberships of both the Kane County and the Escalante Cattlemen's Association reviewed the budgets, but did not make any substantive changes.
entities.stance_basis: reviewed ranch budget sheets without substantive changes; no explicit stance on the overall EIS attributed

entities.quote: The Division of Wildlife Resources reported Peregrine Falcon sightings near Kanab on December 15, 1979, and at Lone Rock in Wahweap Bay during 1979 and 1980 in the Glen Canyon National Recreation Area.
entities.stance_basis: provided factual wildlife sighting information; no explicit stance on the proposal

entities.quote: The cuts that we have received. I'm speaking specifically about the Circle Cliffs Allotment. Twelve years ago, the BLM furnished us a management plan which was signed by the BLM and the ranchers in that area. At this time we agreed to take a 68-percent cut, and they would reseed three allotments, put in a water system, and as of to date, the BLM has reseeded two allotments and they have got a half a water system in. And on top of that, they want to give us another 43-percent reduction.
entities.stance_basis: Objects to additional 43-percent reduction on top of prior 68-percent cut, citing BLM's failure to fulfill its side of the management plan

entities.quote: I feel that this environmental impact study is discriminatory against the Circle Cliffs permit holders, due to the fact that the reduction is based on actual use rather than preference right as in the other allotments.
entities.stance_basis: Asserts the EIS is discriminatory against Circle Cliffs permit holders and calls for return to pre-AMP allotment management

entities.quote: We run on Big Bown Bench - Big Bown Bench has been on the comeback now for 7 years. They have come off in the spring, and I feel this is a good enough cut without taking another 40 percent cut.
entities.stance_basis: Opposes additional 40-percent cut on Big Bown Bench Allotment given 7-year recovery trend

entities.quote: I feel that any cut that has taken place or should take place in the future if there are future cuts, that someone has to reckon with these farmers and ranchers that have spent money knowingly by all parties for permit rights, need to be reimbursed to the individual.
entities.stance_basis: Demands reimbursement for permit rights value lost due to grazing cuts

entities.quote: Now I would request that there be serious consideration given to a moratorium that should be honored by all parties, with no cuts until all improvements that have already been planned are completed and given a year or two to flourish.
entities.stance_basis: Requests moratorium on cuts until planned improvements are completed and evaluated

entities.quote: I don't think you've taken into consideration the economic effects on the communities where cuts are to be made. They haven't been considered at all, only to the cattlemen's standpoint.
entities.stance_basis: Objects that community economic impacts have not been adequately considered in the EIS

entities.quote: I think it is time the BLM adopt Section 4 where permittees could develop water cooperatively with BLM and also other needed projects.
entities.stance_basis: Requests BLM adopt cooperative development approach under Section 4

entities.quote: I think the trend plots used by the BLM are too few and usually located in areas where cattle move to water, not showing the true picture of range trends.
entities.stance_basis: Challenges validity of BLM trend plot methodology used to support management decisions

entities.quote: Since 1960 on our allotment, Lower Cattle Allotment, we've taken a 45-percent reduction plus 2 month's time. With the present proposal of 26 percent added together, that makes a total of 71 percent since 1960.
entities.stance_basis: Objects to cumulative 71-percent reduction since 1960 on Lower Cattle Allotment

entities.role: Utah State Agricultural College professor, cited by Speaker Number 10
entities.quote: I also think annual plants should be used in determining carrying capacity, and this was stated to us by Professor Bowns from Utah State Agricultural College when we made a range tour with your group.
entities.stance_basis: Cited as supporting inclusion of annual plants in carrying capacity determinations

entities.quote: BLM believes it is best for the rangeland user to have the right to request a nonrenewable license for any particular year of abundant annuals.
entities.stance_basis: BLM supports its own management approach of nonrenewable licenses for annual forage rather than including annuals in base management plans

entities.quote: In general, the FS has the same multiple use land management policies as BLM: long-term sustained use of the resource for public benefit.
entities.stance_basis: Described as having complementary management policies to BLM requiring close coordination

entities.quote: This alternative was developed by BLM through the planning process and evolved from specific multiple use recommendations made in the MFP (BLM Planning Process section, Chapter 1). This alternative is currently preferred by BLM.
entities.stance_basis: BLM identifies Alternative 5 (Rangeland Management Recommendation) as its currently preferred alternative

entities.role: plaintiff in federal court suit against BLM
entities.quote: Further, this EIS is responsive to a suit filed in Federal Court, 1973, by Natural Resources Defense Council et al., alleging that BLM's programmatic grazing EIS did not comply with the National Environmental Policy Act (42 United States Code 4321 et seq.).
entities.stance_basis: Filed suit alleging BLM's programmatic grazing EIS did not comply with NEPA

entities.quote: In general, the FS has the same multiple use land management policies as BLM: long-term sustained use of the resource for public benefit.
entities.stance_basis: Described as having complementary management policies; coordination required but no specific stance on alternatives attributed

entities.role: state wildlife management agency providing data for EIS
entities.quote: Prior stable numbers for deer (17,044 head) and bighorn sheep (150 head) were supplied by UDWR and were used to allocate wildlife forage in the MFP (table 1-2).
entities.stance_basis: Provided wildlife population data used in planning; considering bighorn sheep reintroduction dependent on BLM management outcomes

entities.role: land manager of adjacent NPS units including GCNRA and Bryce Canyon
entities.quote: The NPS is responsible for the administration of national parks, monuments, and GCNRA. BLM has responsibility for management of livestock grazing in GCNRA. The recreation resources of Bryce Canyon National Park and GCNRA could be affected, depending on livestock season and level of use adjustments and the location, type, and extent of vegetation treatments on public lands.
entities.stance_basis: Identified as potentially affected agency; wilderness designation decisions could affect BLM grazing management

entities.quote: Individuals, Groups, or Agencies Attending
entities.stance_basis: Attended multiple scoping meetings (June 27, September 12, September 27-28, 1979) indicating engagement with the planning process

entities.role: livestock operators and public commenters at scoping meetings
entities.quote: Individuals, Groups, or Agencies Attending
entities.stance_basis: Attended multiple scoping meetings; reduced rancher income identified as a significant scoping issue

entities.role: cooperating agency with ranch plans for private lands
entities.quote: SCS efforts are primarily directed toward stabilization of the soil and watershed resources and increasing the productive capability of private land.
entities.stance_basis: Coordinating agency; changes in public land management may alter existing SCS ranch plan designs

entities.role: cooperating agency; informal Section 7 consultation participant
entities.quote: Informal consultation has been initiated in accordance with Section 7 of the Endangered Species Act of 1973. This consultation began early in the multiple use planning stages and was used in the development of the alternatives.
entities.stance_basis: Participated in Section 7 consultation informing alternatives; no specific stance on preferred alternative attributed

entities.quote: Individuals, Groups, or Agencies Attending
entities.stance_basis: Listed as attending a scoping meeting on June 11, 1979 in Cedar City, Utah

entities.quote: Individuals, Groups, or Agencies Attending
entities.stance_basis: Listed as attending a scoping meeting on September 27-28, 1979 in Escalante, Utah

entities.quote: The improvement would be slow, probably negligible in the short term (Sosebee et al., 1977; McLean and Tisdale, 1972).
entities.stance_basis: cited for slow recovery rate of ranges in poor condition

entities.quote: Studies in western Utah have shown that the recovery rate of ranges in poor condition is slow because of low precipitation and a limited growing period (Cook, 1971).
entities.stance_basis: cited for slow recovery rate of ranges in poor condition

entities.quote: According to Hickey (1966), the fastest way to improve deteriorated rangeland and have sufficient cover for natural reseeding is through complete growing season deferment.
entities.stance_basis: cited in support of complete growing season deferment for rangeland improvement

entities.quote: An improvement in plant vigor would not be expected to result in an increase in forage production in the short term because Cook and Child (1971) found that plants harvested moderately during late spring or in winter and late spring still differed significantly in vigor measurements from untreated plants, even after 7 years of protection.
entities.stance_basis: cited for slow short-term forage production improvement even after protection

entities.quote: Improvement would be rapid but would level off in the long term as the areas stabilized (Duff, 1978).
entities.stance_basis: cited for riparian vegetation improvement pattern under reduced utilization

entities.quote: Paulsen (1975) recommends (on southwest rangeland) use of most browse species at 30 to 35 percent of the current year's twig growth and 40 percent for perennial grass.
entities.stance_basis: cited for recommended utilization rates to maintain forage plant vigor

entities.quote: Hutchings (1954) states that improvement in poor condition rangeland can be achieved by grazing the better forage plants at a rate lower than what is recommended for good condition rangeland.
entities.stance_basis: cited for lower grazing rates needed to improve poor condition rangeland

entities.quote: These systems would provide periodic rest during the critical growing season which would allow improved seed production and establishment of seedlings (Hormay, 1970).
entities.stance_basis: cited in support of rest rotation grazing systems for seed production and seedling establishment

entities.quote: Schmutz (1973) indicates that deferred rotation systems improve plant vigor and seedling establishment, and result in more uniform grazing.
entities.stance_basis: cited for benefits of deferred rotation management on vegetation

entities.quote: Buwai and Trlica (1977) also indicate that it may be necessary to give important forage plants periodic rest to insure their productivity.
entities.stance_basis: cited for necessity of periodic rest for forage plant productivity

entities.quote: Treatments occuring in pinyon-juniper sites may, however, suffer reinvasion by undesirable species within 15 years (Tausch and Tueller, 1977) and would require retreatment.
entities.stance_basis: cited for reinvasion risk in pinyon-juniper treatment sites

entities.quote: At least 3 years could elapse before production of many desirable species would be back to pretreatment levels (Vallentine, 1971).
entities.stance_basis: cited for post-burn recovery timeline for desirable species

entities.quote: Linne (1978) also indicates that undesirable species (rabbitbrush) may increase after burning.
entities.stance_basis: cited for risk of undesirable species increase after burning

entities.quote: Nielsen and Henkley (1975) report up to a 100-percent kill of sagebrush by fire.
entities.stance_basis: cited for sagebrush kill rate by fire

entities.quote: As Reynolds and Martin (1968) indicate, sustained high production of perennial grass requires grazing of desirable plants to the proper degree at appropriate times and the optimum distribution of livestock.
entities.stance_basis: cited for conditions required for sustained perennial grass production

entities.quote: Improvement in forage condition would occur because as Anderson (1969) indicates, the effect of proper grazing use on key forage increases or maintains vigor, enhances seedling establishment, and causes an eventual thickening of the stand.
entities.stance_basis: cited for benefits of proper grazing use on forage condition

entities.quote: More substantial improvement in condition classes would not be expected since the extent that vegetation composition would be altered to improve condition classes would be limited because of the generally unresponsive nature of pinyon-juniper and sagebrush to management (Springfield, 1976).
entities.stance_basis: cited for limited management responsiveness of pinyon-juniper and sagebrush

entities.quote: Jenkins (1972) further cites a benefit of grazing systems, suggesting that periodic rest may be required to offset the selective grazing pressure placed on desirable forage species.
entities.stance_basis: cited for benefits of periodic rest in grazing systems

entities.quote: Many authors such as Pechanec, et al., (1965), Dwyer (1975), and Potter et al., (1967) indicate that natural improvement in mature pinyon-juniper and sagebrush areas is limited.
entities.stance_basis: cited for limited natural improvement in pinyon-juniper and sagebrush areas

entities.quote: Many authors such as Pechanec, et al., (1965), Dwyer (1975), and Potter et al., (1967) indicate that natural improvement in mature pinyon-juniper and sagebrush areas is limited.
entities.stance_basis: cited for limited natural improvement in pinyon-juniper and sagebrush areas

entities.quote: Many authors such as Pechanec, et al., (1965), Dwyer (1975), and Potter et al., (1967) indicate that natural improvement in mature pinyon-juniper and sagebrush areas is limited.
entities.stance_basis: cited for limited natural improvement in pinyon-juniper and sagebrush areas

entities.quote: One study (VanPoolen and Lacey, 1979) indicates that production increases an average of 13 percent.
entities.stance_basis: cited for average forage production increase under management systems

entities.quote: Martin and Whitfield (1973) also indicate that grazing systems are an added technique for improving and maintaining forage production and in the long term usually permit increased levels of stocking and increased production per acre.
entities.stance_basis: cited for long-term benefits of grazing systems on forage production

entities.quote: Research by Dunford (1949), Smeins (1975). Lusby (1970), and Thomas et al. (1979) indicates that grazing at greater than moderate utilization (40 to 60 percent), grazing in gullies (versus nongrazing) and "improper grazing practices" in riparian areas would cause accelerated erosion and soil loss.
entities.stance_basis: cited for accelerated erosion from heavy grazing

entities.quote: Research by Dunford (1949), Smeins (1975). Lusby (1970), and Thomas et al. (1979) indicates that grazing at greater than moderate utilization (40 to 60 percent), grazing in gullies (versus nongrazing) and "improper grazing practices" in riparian areas would cause accelerated erosion and soil loss.
entities.stance_basis: cited for accelerated erosion from heavy grazing

entities.quote: Research by Dunford (1949), Smeins (1975). Lusby (1970), and Thomas et al. (1979) indicates that grazing at greater than moderate utilization (40 to 60 percent), grazing in gullies (versus nongrazing) and "improper grazing practices" in riparian areas would cause accelerated erosion and soil loss.
entities.stance_basis: cited for accelerated erosion from heavy grazing

entities.quote: Research by Dunford (1949), Smeins (1975). Lusby (1970), and Thomas et al. (1979) indicates that grazing at greater than moderate utilization (40 to 60 percent), grazing in gullies (versus nongrazing) and "improper grazing practices" in riparian areas would cause accelerated erosion and soil loss.
entities.stance_basis: cited for accelerated erosion from heavy grazing

entities.quote: Branson et al. (1975) state that "grazing animals remove protective plant cover and compact the soil surface" and that "both of these actions affect the infiltration rate".
entities.stance_basis: cited for negative soil impacts of livestock grazing

entities.quote: Van Poolen and Lacey (1979) indicate that herbage production responses to livestock number reductions were more significant than those attributable to grazing systems.
entities.stance_basis: cited for greater effectiveness of livestock number reductions vs. grazing systems

entities.quote: The 8,078 acres of burning proposed in this alternative would have increased soil erosion for at least 1 or 2 years, until vegetation cover reached preburn levels and percent bare soil was reduced (Ffolliott and Thorud, 1975).
entities.stance_basis: cited for post-burn soil erosion increase

entities.quote: This would occur as a result of reducing surface litter, destroying soil organic matter, and subsequently exposing large amounts of bare soil to the erosive effects of summer rain storms while the newly seeded plants were becoming established (Wells et al., 1979).
entities.stance_basis: cited for soil erosion mechanisms following burning treatment

entities.quote: Lusby (1979) looked at the effects of converting sagebrush cover to grass on four small watersheds in Colorado and determined that sediment yield from the two converted watersheds was reduced nearly 80 percent from those of the two untreated watersheds.
entities.stance_basis: cited for sediment yield reduction following sagebrush-to-grass conversion

entities.quote: Studies by Blackburn and Skau (1974) and Gifford and Busby (1974) have shown that only minor reductions of sediment production resulted from plowing.
entities.stance_basis: cited for minor sediment reduction from plowing treatments

entities.quote: Studies by Blackburn and Skau (1974) and Gifford and Busby (1974) have shown that only minor reductions of sediment production resulted from plowing.
entities.stance_basis: cited for minor sediment reduction from plowing treatments

entities.quote: Only when the slash debris is windrowed following chaining would there be potential for increased runoff and sediment yields (Gifford et al., 1970).
entities.stance_basis: cited for sediment yield risk from windrowing after chaining

entities.quote: Recent research on the effects of rest rotation systems in riparian areas indicates that the increased trampling and trailing potential of this system may accelerate streambank erosion and instability (Meehan and Platts, 1978).
entities.stance_basis: cited for streambank erosion risk from rest rotation systems in riparian areas

entities.quote: In addition, Busby (1979) states that rest rotation systems "seem to work well when precipitation exceeds 15 to 20 inches per year and is predictably distributed. Both of these requirements are necessary to insure that the rest period will result in soil and vegetation recovery."
entities.stance_basis: cited for precipitation requirements for effective rest rotation systems

entities.quote: Sturges (1975) indicates that few methods of sagebrush treatment permanently eradicate the brush.
entities.stance_basis: cited for limited permanence of sagebrush treatment methods

entities.quote: Heady and Bartolome (1977) also found that the apparent reinvasion on treated sites was from surviving plants.
entities.stance_basis: cited for sagebrush reinvasion from surviving plants on treated sites

entities.quote: However, a review of the literature on the impacts of rest rotation systems on watershed values (infiltration, sedimentation, etc.) has shown a definite lack of research in this area (Gifford and Hawkirs, 1976).
entities.stance_basis: cited for lack of research on rest rotation systems and watershed values

entities.quote: Research by Dunford (1949), Smeins (1975) and Johnston (1962) indicates that grazing at greater than moderate utilization would cause accelerated erosion and soil loss.
entities.stance_basis: cited for accelerated erosion from heavy grazing

entities.quote: Studies of water samples taken from unprotected streams which were directly sprayed with 2,4,D showed concentrations of the chemicals below the levels recommended for public water supplies by the Environmental Protection Agency, and much below the 50-percent lethal dose values for fish
entities.stance_basis: referenced as standard-setter for public water supply chemical levels

entities.quote: Water repellent soils (DeBano, 1969) may also be an important factor controlling runoff and erosion following fire.
entities.stance_basis: cited for water repellent soils as factor in post-fire runoff and erosion

entities.quote: However, the extent of these changes in water quality due to burning and seeding treatment would depend on initial site characteristics, total area burned, post-burn storm characteristics, and seeding success (Gifford et al., 1975).
entities.stance_basis: cited for factors affecting water quality changes from burning treatment

entities.quote: Thus, downstream coliform bacteria levels would increase during periods of grazing and would decline when livestock are removed (Johnson et al., 1978).
entities.stance_basis: cited for coliform bacteria levels in relation to livestock grazing

entities.quote: Especially critical in this regard are the steep, highly incised gullies prevalent throughout the region that are subject to natural geologic erosion (Heede, 1976).
entities.stance_basis: cited for natural geologic erosion in steep gullies

entities.quote: However, the removal of the soil moisture competition of this vegetation type may increase the amount of soil moisture available to understory vegetation (Skau, 1964; Tausch and Tueller, 1977).
entities.stance_basis: cited for soil moisture availability increase after overstory removal

entities.role: federal wildlife agency, consulted on threatened and endangered species
entities.quote: No adverse impacts to these species as a result of implementing any of the six alternatives were identified during informal consultations with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service (John Gill, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, personal communication, 1979).
entities.stance_basis: found no adverse impacts to threatened/endangered species under any alternative

entities.role: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service representative
entities.quote: No adverse impacts to these species as a result of implementing any of the six alternatives were identified during informal consultations with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service (John Gill, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, personal communication, 1979).
entities.stance_basis: found no adverse impacts to threatened/endangered species under any alternative

entities.role: BLM district office, source of 1975-79 Range Survey data
entities.quote: According to the 1975-79 Range Survey (BLM Cedar City District Office) more than adequate forage is available to deer.
entities.stance_basis: cited as source of range survey data used in impact analysis, no project stance attributed

entities.role: state wildlife agency, proposed bighorn sheep transplant
entities.quote: A bighorn sheep transplant in this area is proposed by UDWR.
entities.stance_basis: proposed bighorn sheep transplant; on-the-ground examinations by BLM and UDWR personnel referenced in management context

entities.role: wildlife management body, source of sage grouse habitat guidelines
entities.quote: Treatments that would occur in sage grouse habitat would conform to guidelines which were developed for sage grouse habitat (Western States Sage Grouse Committee, 1968).
entities.stance_basis: guidelines cited as standard to be followed for sage grouse habitat treatments

entities.quote: The Bureau of Land Management, hereinafter referred to as the Bureau, is preparing the Kanab/Escalante Grazing Management Environmental Impact Statement (Kanab/Escalante EIS) under the provisions of the National Environmental Policy Act of 1969.
entities.stance_basis: BLM is the lead agency preparing and implementing the grazing management EIS and its proposed alternatives

entities.role: State Historic Preservation Officer, signatory to MOU
entities.quote: The Arizona State Historic Preservation Office, hereinafter referred to as the State is interested in assuring that cultural values in Arizona be protected.
entities.stance_basis: Entered MOU with BLM to protect cultural values conditional on specified protective procedures being correctly implemented

entities.role: State Historic Preservation Officer, signatory to MOU
entities.quote: The Utah State Historic Preservation Office, hereinafter referred to as the SHPO, is interested in assuring that cultural values in Utah be protected.
entities.stance_basis: Entered MOU with BLM to protect cultural values conditional on specified protective procedures being correctly implemented

entities.entity: Executive Secretary of the Utah Air Conservation Commission
entities.role: Utah Air Conservation Commission, regulatory authority for burning
entities.quote: No burning would take place until permission would be granted by the Executive Secretary of the Utah Air Conservation Commission or his authorized representative (State of Utah Air Conservation Regulations, 10-13'78).
entities.stance_basis: Burning requires prior permission from this official, indicating conditional approval authority over burning activities

entities.entity: Bureau of Land Management (BLM), Cedar City District
entities.role: lead agency preparing the Draft EIS
entities.quote: the BLM preferred alternative is Alternative 5, Rangeland Management Recommendation.
entities.stance_basis: BLM explicitly identifies Alternative 5 as its preferred alternative
